Our objective for this module is to give you a framework to create a succinct, compelling, and persuasive description of the value you can create for a minimum viable audience as it relates to the jobs they want to accomplish, pains they want to avoid, and or gains they want to acquire. To do that, we're going to borrow some ideas from our friends at Sterling and Stone, and introduce you to a TDB-specific writing marathon inspired by one of our favorite books of all time, Accidental Genius. Since October 3, 2011, Sterling and Stone co-founders, and writing partners, Sean Platt, Johnny Truant, and David Wright have published more than 100 books and written more than 6 million words. They've also written more than 1.5 million words in a single year. To put that into perspective, the entire Harry Potter series is 1,084,170 words. Sean, Johnny, and David have developed a step-by-step story beats process that will distil and modify, slightly, for our purposes. They're writing fiction, upwards of 100k words per book, we're writing much shorter non-fiction emails, marketing messages, landing pages, ads, and offers. While there are plenty of differences between the two, the underlying concepts for writing story beats applies to both their work and ours. If you'd like to learn more about their method in its entirety, especially if you write fiction, we highly recommend their Udemy course How to Plan and Outline novels using Scrivener. One caveat, you don't need to use Scrivener to get a lot of value from the course. We don't. Story beats are, in Sean's words, sketches, signposts, and seeds of ideas you, as the writer, can expand on. Beats are not gospel, their job is to get you going through the draft. In simpler terms, think about beats as an outline of ideas. The goal initially is to get those ideas out of your head and onto a page without getting wrapped up in concerns about writing. Beats aren't prose, they're succinct descriptions of ideas. And aside by Andre, making magic. Reading this is not required for this lesson. This won't resonate with everyone, but if it resonates with you, the experience can feel like making magic, or like you've been sent a gift from the gods. In the book Writing as a Way of Being, Robert Yagelski explains the difference between self-expression, through writing, and expression of the self, through writing. Writing is a form of self-expression. You have a self separate from the writing process, you have thoughts in your head, and writing is a tool to bring those thoughts to an audience. This is the form of writing we all know about. Expression of the self, through writing, is when the self exists in the process of writing, it's a becoming that happens in the physical process of writing that creates thought itself at that moment. When you read it like that, it's hard to make sense of what that feels like or what it even means. Intellectually, yup, it makes sense, but you'll likely still be left with, but yeah, I still don't really get it. Until you experience it and literally see the magic happen in front of your eyes. Then it's like, OD hash CK, what just happened. I was gifted with some of the magic earlier this week. When the muse shows up like this, it's hard to explain the feeling of awe. Case in point, I've been working on a pleasure project with my writing coach, Ali Da, the past few weeks. I'm writing a novel or novella. It's urban fantasy, which seemed like a fun idea at the time. I had an idea of the protagonist, our reluctant hero, but the antagonist, his slash or motivation, the plot, all of the import elements of a story was a complete mystery to me. So Ali Da had me for writing, write any scene about the protagonist, which I did, no issues there. It was fun. Some details emerge that I didn't know about. Then Ali Da, the wily old Fox, said I should do the same for the antagonist. I could see what she was up to. So I gave it a shot. But other than some elements, I already had an idea about nothing magical revealed itself. I still didn't know anything new. Last Friday, we talked it through a bit more, and she said, I want you to write the opening scene to your story. Fantastic, I thought. Not. We deconstructed the opening scene to orphan black on YouTube. It's a three minute scene that is full of unanswered questions. Do that, she said. Use that as inspiration. Write, write, easy, no problem. The writing lessons we'll unpack in this lesson are essentially what I did this week for the writing I'm doing for Ali Da. 6.5 minutes of breathing exercise I got from a friend, 25 deep breaths. 30 minute timer, I use a 30 minute sand timer pictured below. Write. No stopping. Note, if anyone is interested in the breathing exercise for writing, post in the comments. I'll then ask our friend, Mike, if he's okay with us sharing it in this group. I literally had no idea what I was doing to write. I wrote a scene on Monday in the time. The sun was almost set as a purple hue enveloped the warm city. People were out, drawn to the connections of social circles and sweaty bars, or were being expressed back onto the streets in noisy laughter to find the next watering hole. Then, in the moment of writing the above exposition, a character revealed himself to me, and I pulled the thread. First, I thought it was my antagonist, so I continued to write until the sands of time ran out. At this point, I presumed it was my antagonist, but I didn't have any details yet. Tuesday morning, I opened a new page, and after the breathing, flipped the sand glass over and wrote the start of a new scene. Marty entered the lobby of an all-glass building in a district of the city known for the highest concentration of bulldog law firms. If you needed a lawyer, this was not the place to seek counsel, unless you met nine or 10 figure corporation. Marty was feeling a little delicate from the night before. And so it unfolded. After 30 minutes, I stopped. I have no idea who Marty is, the name just showed up as I started to write. Wednesday morning, same process, new scene. The two friends found a table at the back of a noisy Starbucks. Jen, I don't know what to do something weird has just happened, she said, her blue eyes wide as they darted left and right. She wore a hazelnut milk mustache. Curly blonde bangs hung wildly. She brushed a stray curl behind her ear, took another swig of her grand hazelnut cappuccino. She continued. And as the sand was nearing the end of my session, at that moment, another insight revealed itself, which I hadn't seen, or even considered. This week, I experienced three moments of expression of the self through longhand fair writing. But the really cool thing, kinda like in the movie, inception, each new scene revealed an interconnected puzzle piece of the full story, as I work to assemble it in real time through my writing. This sh h t works, folks, do the work and be open to what will happen. Back to the lesson. When Sean, Johnny, and David write stories, they rely on four documents that inform their writing. Those documents are a single sentence description of the book. Elevator pitch, a one to two paragraph description of the book. Rough story arc, a summary of each stage of the book. Simple synopsis beats on speed. Everything in the story told in a rat a tat tat, this happened then this happened format. For our purposes, we're going to use three of those documents, modified slightly, and add one of our own. The hook, a single sentence that succinctly and persuasively combines the audience you serve with the value you create. The frame, a 300 to 500 work summary that explains who you serve, the value you create, and why that value is so important to your audience. The manifesto, 1000 to 2000 words that dimensionalizes the value you create in functional, social, and emotional terms. Manifestos can be public facing, if that fits your needs, or it can be an internal reference document that informs your marketing messaging. The dossier, your virtual shoebox of ideas captured in a single document. These can be quotes, stories, beliefs, evidence, relevant facts, etc. A dossier collects your story beats into one staging area to draw ideas from. It's important to mention that you're going to create these documents in reverse order, from broad dossier to narrow single sentence hook. Sean Platt explains it this way. Rather than build outward from a simple idea, you start by getting your thoughts onto a page and then making that increasingly precise. Easy, right? We know what you're thinking, sounds great guys, but how am I supposed to actually do that work? That's where writing marathon will help. Our TDB writing marathon is inspired by accidental genius. We can't recommend that book enough, the subtitle of that book is using writing to generate your best ideas, insight, and content, and that's exactly what we're going to do. Here are the steps. You don't have to follow these steps exactly, but we encourage you to try this version as written before making any adjustments. Once you've done that, you'll be in a better position to make any tweaks specific to your individual needs or not. Here's what you'll need for this exercise. Pens that are easy to write with no fountain pens, pencils, mechanical pencils, or anything that will interfere with the writing process for seven minutes, nonstop. Paper. This is an analog exercise, not digital. Trust us. Highlighter. Timer, your phone alarms and electronic assistance like Alexa, Google Assistant, and Siri are fine, or just Google Timer. The writing process. Start writing the moment the timer starts and do not stop until the alarm sounds. Do not look at the clock, check the time, daydream, eat a snack, take a sip of coffee, or tea, or even pause when you're writing. Your pen never leaves the page. If you can't think about anything to write then write, I can't think of anything to write until you can, or Sean and Andre are idiots, I want to punch them in the face, Sean and Andre are idiots, I want to punch them in the face. Write whatever comes to mind without any concerns about formatting, grammar, proper punctuation, etc. No editing, just write, write, write. Nonstop. You're going to write in response to seven prompts. The first session will be seven minutes. All other sessions will be five minutes. You will write all seven sessions in one sitting, no breaks. That's why it's called a marathon. Prompt number one, seven minutes, if you are, insert description of your MBA, here's how I can help you. Accomplish something. Avoid problems. Get what you want. Write that prompt, in whichever of the three options apply to your specific idea, at the top of a blank sheet of paper, start your timer, and write continuously for seven minutes without taking your pen off the paper. No editing. For example, if you are interested in personal knowledge management, here's how I can help you generate ideas, high quality ideas, without locking yourself in a room for weeks at a time, quickly and dependably, creativity on demand. Immediately after you finished writing for seven minutes, read what you wrote and highlight anything that stands out for you as interesting, valuable, persuasive, or well written. In particular, you're looking for references to anything meaningful you've identified that you can help your MBA accomplish, avoid, and or acquire. Prompt number two, five minutes, I can help you accomplish x by be specific. For example, if you identified learning conversational Italian as something meaningful, your MBA wants to accomplish in your first seven minute writing session, prompt number two might be I can help you learn conversational Italian by then write whatever comes to mind nonstop no editing give yourself a three to five minute break after each round of writing then move on to the next prompt number three five minutes I can help you avoid x by x is a pain or pains you identified in the first seven minute session. For example, if leaning conversation Italian is what you're helping an audience accomplish, your prompt would be something like I can help you avoid spending hours per day practicing Italian grammar. That'll get you started, then just go with whatever appears after that. Don't edit or try to direct where your thoughts go. Let your thoughts reveal themselves. Prompt number four, five minutes, I can help you acquire x by x is a gain you identified in the first seven minute session. For example, I can help you acquire conversational Italian fluency by again, don't direct or edit capture whatever arises prompts five, six, and seven, five minutes each. What you the audience want to accomplish is important because what you want to avoid is important because what you want to acquire is important because at the end of this process you will likely feel exhausted. If you followed the outline above, you will have written nonstop for 37 minutes by passing or at least undermining you inner critic and inner editor tapping into your subconscious along the way. You should have a stack of paper with a lot of writing. Most of that writing will be junk. However, you'll likely find the seeds of a some really good ideas. Those seeds are the raw material for your beats. Grab your highlighter and review what you've written highlight anything you notice that is well written persuasive, impactful, important, or meaningful. After you've done that, capture all of those ideas in a digital dossier. This can be a simple document or a more complex outlining tool like work flowy, dino list or Rome. Don't worry about organizing or editing yet. Just get the full list of ideas into a single location. Prompts one to four identified the what and how of your initial idea to validate prompts five to seven identified the why combined, you should have a very good start on the ingredients for a manifesto. Take some time to polish the beats in your dossier so they're clear and concise. Next, you'll assemble those beats into a coherent manifesto. You don't need to use all of them and you can add ideas that emerge to our manifesto prompt questions. What is the big idea? Attention. What is the thesis statement controlling idea? What is the context authority, empathy, evidence and explanation to shape beliefs? What is an insight? I want the reader to discover without having to explicitly spell it out, unifying theory of two plus two conclude comes full circle and end with a call to action. What is the call to action? I want the reader to make keep refining that manifesto until it reads well sounds right when read aloud and explains the idea you want to validate with precision and clarity. Then you're going to move on to the frame strip the manifesto down to the bare elements. Who do you help? How do you help them? Why is that meaningful to them? Finally, distill your frame to a single powerful hook one to two sentences, one is better. There are lots of formulas for this and easy one to use is. I help my audience achieve their desire without common problem. For example, our one sentence description for TLB might be. We help creators make money online without feeling like used car salesman. One final note about the process, give yourself some time for this exercise, but not too much time. We recommend a four step approach. Step one, identify the seeds of our idea in broad strokes after completing the work in the discover process and validate part one. You don't need to know the details. Just make sure you have some general direction. Step two, complete the TDB writing marathon, start to finish during your peak creative time. We're at our creative best in the morning and that's when we do this work. If you're a night owl or your best creative time is in the afternoon, plan accordingly. Step three, take a mental break for at least 24 to 48 hours. You may be tempted to go directly from the writing marathon to creating your simple synopsis. We think you'll get better results if you let the ideas you identified in the writing marathon percolate for a day or two. Step four, compile your dossier first, then write a draft of your manifesto. Polish that over hours or maybe even days. Then refine the manifesto into a frame and distill the frame to a hook. This may take a few days to get it right. We recommend that you limit yourself to three days or less. That seems to be the creative Goldilocks sweet spot for this work. Once you have these four documents you'll be ready to share your ideas with potential prospects to get their feedback. We'll explain how to do that next in Validate, Part 3. Questions or feedback, please use the comments below. Use the private accountability community to have discussions with your peers and share your insights with them. We strongly suggest once you've done the exercises in Part 2 that you share your work within the community with your peers. Finally, enjoy the process, lean into it, be open to what will emerge. Andre and Sean.
